transcript: Mount Fuji Mount Fuji is Japan's tallest mountain, standing at 3,776 meters, or 12,380 feet. It is an active volcano, but a low risk, with its last eruption in 1707. It is located near the Pacific coast of central Honshu, only 100 kilometers, or 62 miles, from Tokyo, the capital of Japan, and can be seen from there on a clear day. The summit of Mount Fuji has a tundra climate, and the temperature is very low at the high altitude, with its cone covered in snow for several months in the year. The mountain is a very popular tourist site, and over 200,000 people climb to the summit every year, in particular during the summertime, when huts provide supplies and rest to hikers. Throughout history, the mountain has featured in Japanese artwork, including woodblock prints. It is also a very important spiritual symbol of Japan, having been a sacred site for practitioners of Shinto. Many shrines are dotted around the mountain. This show is a production of the Center for Autism and Related Dis, who is the goddess of Mount Fuji and all volcanoes. Mount Fuji has been recognized as an inspiration for artists, poets, and and pilgrimages for, and was granted World Heritage status in 2013.
